missedConsequences.Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy: Patient sent home with 'ovarian cyst.' Returns 2 hours later in hemorrhagic shock. Despite emergent surgery, requires massive transfusion. Nearly dies on the table.
missedConsequences.Ectopic Pregnancy: Without prompt diagnosis, ectopic ruptures in waiting room. Delay to OR increases blood loss from 500mL to 2000mL.
missedConsequences.Ovarian Torsion: Torsed ovary becomes necrotic after 6-hour delay. Requires oophorectomy, affecting future fertility.

commonPitfalls: NOT GETTING A PREGNANCY TEST - this is the cardinal error. Pregnancy test in ALL reproductive-age women with abdominal pain, no exceptions
Being reassured by 'she uses contraception' - no contraception is 100% effective
Missing Kehr's sign (shoulder pain) - referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation by blood indicates hemoperitoneum
Waiting for type-specific blood before surgery - in unstable patient, give O-negative and go to OR
Attributing tachycardia to 'anxiety' - tachycardia in this context is blood loss until proven otherwise

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Reproductive-age female with RLQ pain, missed period, and risk factors (prior PID). Tachycardia and borderline low BP suggest possible rupture. MUST be at top of differential.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Cyst Rupture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common cause of acute pelvic pain in young women. Can cause significant pain but usually self-limited.

allDiagnoses.name: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Tachycardia and near-syncope suggest hemodynamic compromise. Can be rapidly fatal without intervention.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Surgical emergency - ovary can become necrotic within hours. Sudden onset severe pain with nausea is classic.

allDiagnoses.name: Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: RLQ pain classic for appendicitis. Must differentiate from gynecologic causes.

allDiagnoses.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)
allDiagnoses.rationale: History of prior PID increases ectopic risk. Current presentation less typical for acute PID (no discharge, no fever).

allDiagnoses.name: UTI/Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect dysuria, frequency, and flank pain for pyelo.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually bilateral cramping with diarrhea, not localized RLQ pain.

allDiagnoses.name: Kidney Stone
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect colicky flank pain radiating to groin, hematuria.

female-26-ectopic.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started yesterday and it's gotten a lot worse. It's in my right lower belly, pretty sharp. Probably an 8 out of 10 right now. It's constant - doesn't come and go. Nothing makes it better, and moving around makes it worse. I also felt really lightheaded earlier when I stood up - almost passed out. I've been a little nauseous but no vomiting. My last period... I guess it's been about 7 weeks? I'm usually pretty regular, every 28 days. I am sexually active with my boyfriend. We use condoms but not every time, and I'm not on the pill. I had a chlamydia infection a few years ago that turned into PID - I was in the hospital for that. I have an IUD... well, I had one but it fell out about 2 months ago and I haven't gotten a new one yet."

historyQuestions.text: When did this pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started yesterday as a dull ache. Then this morning it got suddenly a lot worse - that's when I felt like I might pass out. It's been constant since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset with sudden worsening suggests initial tubal distension followed by rupture

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 7 weeks ago. I'm usually regular, every 28 days, so I'm definitely late.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Missed period in reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain = pregnancy until proven otherwise. 7 weeks is classic timing for ectopic to present.

historyQuestions.text: Have you taken a pregnancy test?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I didn't even think about that. I mean, we use condoms... most of the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Every reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain needs pregnancy test regardless of contraception history.

historyQuestions.text: Any vaginal bleeding or spotting?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, I had some light spotting a few days ago. Not like my normal period - just some brownish discharge.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaginal bleeding + missed period + pelvic pain = ectopic pregnancy triad. Spotting is classic for ectopic.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about feeling lightheaded - did you actually pass out?
historyQuestions.answer: When I stood up to go to the bathroom I got really dizzy and my vision went gray. I had to grab the wall. I almost fell but caught myself.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL RED FLAG: Near-syncope suggests hemodynamic compromise, likely from intra-abdominal bleeding. This is ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the PID you had before.
historyQuestions.answer: I was 22. I had chlamydia that I didn't know about, and it spread. I was really sick - high fever, bad pain. They admitted me for IV antibiotics for a few days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of PID is the #1 risk factor for ectopic pregnancy. PID causes tubal scarring that can trap the embryo.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned an IUD that fell out?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I had a copper IUD for about a year but it came out on its own about 2 months ago. I was going to get another one but never made the appointment.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IUD use can increase relative risk of ectopic if pregnancy occurs. Expulsion 2 months ago means she's been without reliable contraception.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your typical periods - how regular are they?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually every 28 days, pretty regular. They last about 5 days, moderate flow. I've never had irregular periods before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing baseline menstrual pattern helps confirm this is truly a missed period, not irregularity

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been pregnant before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, this would be my first pregnancy if I am pregnant. I've never had a miscarriage or abortion.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gravidity/parity important; prior ectopic would greatly increase risk. First pregnancy doesn't rule out ectopic.

historyQuestions.text: Walk me through your contraception use recently.
historyQuestions.answer: After my IUD fell out 2 months ago, we've been using condoms but honestly not every time. Sometimes we use withdrawal. I know that's not great.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inconsistent contraception explains pregnancy despite 'using protection'

historyQuestions.text: Any breast tenderness, fatigue, or morning sickness?
historyQuestions.answer: My breasts have been a little sore, now that you mention it. I've been tired but I thought it was work stress. No real morning sickness.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pregnancy symptoms can be present in ectopic; their presence supports pregnancy diagnosis

historyQuestions.text: How many sexual partners do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my boyfriend. We've been together for about a year.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sexual history for STI risk and social context

historyQuestions.text: Any other sexually transmitted infections besides the chlamydia?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just that one time. I get tested regularly now because of what happened.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior STIs, especially chlamydia, increase ectopic risk through tubal damage

historyQuestions.text: Any pain in your shoulder or upper abdomen?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, my right shoulder has been achy since this morning. I thought I slept on it wrong.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Referred shoulder pain (Kehr's sign) indicates diaphragmatic irritation from blood in the abdomen. Strongly suggests hemoperitoneum.

historyQuestions.text: How did the pain start - sudden or gradual?
historyQuestions.answer: It started as kind of a dull ache yesterday but then got suddenly much worse this morning. That's when I felt like I might pass out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sudden worsening suggests rupture. Timeline fits with ectopic: gradual symptoms as tubal pregnancy grows, then acute when rupture occurs.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea or vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been queasy all day but haven't thrown up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea common in ectopic, appendicitis, and torsion. Non-specific but part of pattern.

historyQuestions.text: Any pain with urination, frequency, or blood in urine?
historyQuestions.answer: No, peeing is normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of urinary symptoms makes UTI and kidney stone less likely.

historyQuestions.text: Any diarrhea, constipation, or blood in stool?
historyQuestions.answer: No, bowels have been normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No GI symptoms makes gastroenteritis less likely.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before emergent surgery and anesthesia

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just some ibuprofen for the pain. I was using the copper IUD until it fell out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication list for anesthesia; NSAIDs may affect bleeding

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Just that PID when I was 22. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Overall health for surgical risk; confirms PID as major risk factor

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never had any surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior abdominal surgery; laparoscopy will be straightforward

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I had breakfast around 8am - just toast and coffee. Haven't felt like eating since.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status critical for emergent surgery

historyQuestions.text: Do you know your blood type?
historyQuestions.answer: I think I'm O positive but I'm not sure.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Blood type for potential transfusion; will confirm with type and screen

historyQuestions.text: Is anyone here with you?
historyQuestions.answer: My boyfriend is in the waiting room. He drove me here.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Support person and consent witness for emergent surgery

occupation_current_job_duties.answer: I work at a retail store. On my feet all day, long shifts.
occupation_current_job_duties.relevance: Standing work with long hours can exacerbate pelvic discomfort; work schedule may delay seeking care.

occupation_current_work_schedule.answer: Irregular hours. Sometimes close one night and open the next. Hard to get time off.
occupation_current_work_schedule.relevance: Difficulty getting time off work can delay healthcare access - important in time-sensitive conditions like ectopic pregnancy.

social_healthcare_access.answer: I have insurance through work but the deductible is high. Haven't seen a gynecologist in a while.
social_healthcare_access.relevance: Limited healthcare access means no prenatal care and delayed recognition of pregnancy - increases risk of missed ectopic.

social_support_system.answer: My boyfriend's been supportive. My parents don't know I'm sexually active.
social_support_system.relevance: Social factors may affect willingness to disclose sexual history or seek care for reproductive health issues.

social_living_situation.answer: I live with roommates. It's been hard to have privacy to deal with all this.
social_living_situation.relevance: Living situation may affect ability to rest and recover; less directly relevant to diagnosis.

reproductive_health_access.answer: I got my IUD at a clinic a couple years ago. Haven't been back since.
reproductive_health_access.relevance: IUD is major risk factor for ectopic if pregnancy occurs; lack of follow-up means no counseling about this risk.

social_stress_factors.answer: Money's tight. I've been picking up extra shifts. Stressed about bills.
social_stress_factors.relevance: Financial stress may delay seeking care and contribute to overall health burden.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: I don't eat great. Skip meals at work, grab fast food on breaks.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Poor nutrition affects overall health but less directly relevant to acute presentation.

physicalExam.finding: BP 102/68 (borderline low), HR 108 (tachycardic), RR 18, Temp 98.8°F, SpO2 99%. Repeat BP after 500mL NS: 106/70.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Tachycardia with borderline low BP indicates early hemorrhagic shock. This patient needs immediate resuscitation and surgery.

physicalExam.finding: Appears uncomfortable and anxious, lying still. Pale skin. Mild diaphoresis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor and diaphoresis are signs of blood loss and sympathetic activation.

physicalExam.finding: Flat, no distension. No visible masses. No surgical scars.
physicalExam.reasoning: Abdominal distension can occur with significant hemoperitoneum but may be absent early.

physicalExam.finding: Significant RLQ and suprapubic tenderness. Mild voluntary guarding. No rebound initially but develops with deeper palpation. Left side less tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: RLQ/pelvic tenderness with developing peritoneal signs indicates pelvic pathology with possible blood irritating peritoneum.

physicalExam.finding: Tenderness present but maximal tenderness is more medial and inferior, toward the pelvis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Location lower and more medial than typical appendicitis. Pelvic source more likely.

physicalExam.finding: Negative - palpation of LLQ does not increase RLQ pain.
physicalExam.reasoning: Negative Rovsing's makes appendicitis less likely.

physicalExam.finding: Psoas sign negative. Obturator sign mildly positive on right.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild obturator sign can be seen with pelvic pathology near the internal obturator muscle.

physicalExam.finding: No CVA tenderness bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence argues against pyelonephritis.

physicalExam.finding: Right adnexal tenderness and fullness. Cervical motion tenderness present (positive chandelier sign). Left adnexa non-tender. No active vaginal bleeding on exam but dark blood in vault.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: CMT + adnexal tenderness + old blood = ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise. CMT occurs when cervix movement pulls on inflamed/bleeding fallopian tube.

physicalExam.finding: Pale conjunctivae. Pale nail beds. Cool extremities.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor and cool extremities indicate blood loss and peripheral vasoconstriction.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test (point-of-care)
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Confirms pregnancy. Combined with RLQ pain and bleeding = ectopic until proven otherwise.

labs.name: Quantitative serum β-hCG
labs.reasoning: Above discriminatory zone (typically 1500-2000) - should see intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound if present. If no IUP seen, strongly suggests ectopic.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 11.2, Hgb 9.8 (LOW - was 13.2 on old labs), Hct 29%, Plt 290
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Hemoglobin 9.8 indicates blood loss. Compared to prior Hgb of 13.2 = significant bleeding.

labs.name: Type and Screen / Crossmatch
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Must have blood available. Ruptured ectopic can rapidly exsanguinate.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Mild metabolic acidosis can indicate tissue hypoperfusion from blood loss.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Important for surgical planning.

imaging.name: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS)
imaging.result: No intrauterine pregnancy (empty uterus). Complex right adnexal mass 3.2cm with ring of fire sign. Moderate free fluid in pelvis (blood). Left ovary normal.
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: No IUP + β-hCG above discriminatory zone + adnexal mass + free fluid = ECTOPIC PREGNANCY with rupture.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Would delay definitive treatment - not indicated
imaging.reasoning: Ultrasound is faster and more specific for ectopic. CT would delay surgical treatment.

procedures.name: Two large-bore IVs
procedures.result: Two 16-gauge IVs placed. 1L NS bolus infusing.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.reasoning: Monitor urine output as marker of resuscitation.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L NS bolused. BP improved to 110/72, HR 98.
treatments.reasoning: Volume resuscitation critical while preparing for surgery.

treatments.name: Transfuse PRBCs
treatments.result: 2 units O-negative given emergently. Type-specific blood now available.
treatments.reasoning: With Hgb 9.8 and ongoing bleeding, transfusion indicated. Don't wait for type-specific in unstable patient.

treatments.name: RhoGAM (if Rh negative)
treatments.result: Patient is Rh positive - not needed.
treatments.reasoning: Must consider in all pregnancy losses. Would give if Rh negative.

consults.name: STAT OB/GYN Surgery consult
consults.result: OB/GYN called to bedside immediately. OR being prepared for emergent laparoscopy vs laparotomy.
consults.reasoning: CRITICAL: Ruptured ectopic requires immediate surgical intervention. Every minute counts.

disposition.name: Emergent OR for surgical management
disposition.result: Patient taken emergently to OR for laparoscopy. Ruptured right ectopic found with 800mL hemoperitoneum. Right salpingectomy performed.
disposition.reasoning: Definitive treatment. Laparoscopy if stable; laparotomy if unstable. Salpingectomy (tube removal) is standard for ruptured ectopic.

disposition.name: Admit for observation
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - patient needs immediate surgery
disposition.reasoning: Observation would allow continued bleeding. Ruptured ectopic is a surgical emergency.

disposition.name: Discharge
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - life-threatening emergency
disposition.reasoning: Patient would die. This is a surgical emergency.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy - Surgical Emergency
keyFindings: Missed period (7 weeks) + RLQ pain + vaginal spotting = ectopic triad
Near-syncope and shoulder pain (Kehr's sign) indicate hemoperitoneum
Tachycardia (HR 108) with borderline low BP (102/68) = early hemorrhagic shock
History of PID - the #1 risk factor for ectopic pregnancy
Positive pregnancy test with β-hCG 4,200 (above discriminatory zone)
Empty uterus on ultrasound with adnexal mass and free fluid = DIAGNOSTIC
Hemoglobin 9.8 (dropped from baseline 13.2) confirms blood loss
teachingPoints: PREGNANCY TEST IN ALL REPRODUCTIVE-AGE WOMEN WITH ABDOMINAL PAIN: This is non-negotiable. Ectopic pregnancy can present atypically and is life-threatening.
ECTOPIC TRIAD: Missed period + vaginal bleeding + pelvic pain. But many patients don't have all three - have a low threshold.
PID IS THE #1 RISK FACTOR: Prior PID causes tubal scarring that can trap the embryo. Other risks: prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD, infertility treatments.
KEHR'S SIGN: Referred shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation by blood. If present with pelvic pain and positive pregnancy test, assume ruptured ectopic.
DISCRIMINATORY ZONE: β-hCG level (usually 1500-2000) above which you should see an intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS. No IUP above this level = likely ectopic.
RUPTURED ECTOPIC = SURGICAL EMERGENCY: Don't wait for type-specific blood. Don't get CT. Get OB/GYN and go to OR. Patients can exsanguinate rapidly.
TIME IS BLOOD: Every minute of delay in a ruptured ectopic means more blood loss. Rapid recognition and resuscitation saves lives.

guidelineReferences.name: ACOG Practice Bulletin: Ectopic Pregnancy 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131(3):e91-e103
keyPoints: Always test for pregnancy in reproductive-age women with abdominal pain
Discriminatory zone: β-hCG 3,510 (TVUS) - no IUP above this suggests ectopic
Ruptured ectopic with hemodynamic instability = immediate surgery
Methotrexate only for stable, unruptured ectopic with β-hCG <5000
Risk factors: PID (strongest), prior ectopic, tubal surgery

guidelineReferences.name: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Ectopic Pregnancy 2016
guidelineReferences.citation: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. BJOG. 2016;123(13):e15-e55
keyPoints: Ectopic pregnancy should be considered in any woman with abdominal pain
TVUS is first-line imaging; TAS if TVUS not available
Free fluid in pelvis + empty uterus + positive β-hCG = ectopic until proven otherwise
Laparoscopy preferred over laparotomy if patient is hemodynamically stable
Anti-D immunoglobulin for Rh-negative women

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Rivera, your pregnancy test is positive. But the pregnancy isn't in your uterus where it should be - it's in your fallopian tube. This is called an ectopic pregnancy. Unfortunately, it has ruptured and is bleeding. I know this is devastating news, but I need you to understand that this is a life-threatening emergency and we need to act right now.
closureScript.plan: We're taking you to surgery immediately. The surgeon will stop the bleeding and remove the ectopic pregnancy. We're giving you blood through your IV to replace what you've lost. The surgical team is already preparing. Your mom is being brought back to see you for a moment before we go.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Right now, we just need you to stay calm and let us take care of you. The team is excellent and this is what they do.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know you have so many questions and fears right now. Let me address the most important one: you will be able to get pregnant again in the future in most cases. We're doing everything we can to preserve your fertility. But right now, the most important thing is your life, and we need to move. We'll have time to talk about everything else after you're safe.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was hemodynamically stable?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same RLQ pain with positive pregnancy test but stable vitals and no free fluid

chiefComplaint.original: I have really bad stomach pain on the right side
chiefComplaint.modified: I have RLQ pain for 2 days. Pregnancy test positive. Stable vitals, no free fluid on ultrasound.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Unruptured ectopic pregnancy - medical management candidate

feedback.correct: Stable unruptured ectopic may be candidate for methotrexate. Criteria: β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5cm, no contraindications to MTX. Close follow-up required - can still rupture.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of stable ectopic. What are the criteria for medical management?
feedback.incorrect: Stable unruptured ectopic: consider METHOTREXATE if: β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5cm, patient reliable for follow-up. Still needs close monitoring - ~10% failure rate. Any instability → surgery.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ruptured ectopic = emergent surgery. Unruptured + stable + meets criteria = methotrexate option. Patient reliability for follow-up is crucial - if any doubt, surgery is safer.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was also an intrauterine pregnancy?
whatIfScenarios.description: IVF patient with IUP seen AND adnexal mass with free fluid

chiefComplaint.original: I have really bad stomach pain on the right side
chiefComplaint.modified: I had IVF 6 weeks ago. Ultrasound shows a pregnancy in my uterus AND a mass in my tube.

feedback.correct: Heterotopic pregnancy (IUP + ectopic) = surgery for ectopic component. CANNOT use methotrexate (would harm IUP). Rare naturally (1:30,000) but up to 1:100 with IVF. Don't assume IUP rules out ectopic in fertility patients!
feedback.partial: Good recognition of heterotopic. Why can't we use methotrexate?
feedback.incorrect: Heterotopic = IUP AND ectopic simultaneously. Much more common with IVF. CANNOT use methotrexate (would terminate IUP). Management is surgical removal of ectopic while preserving IUP. Don't let IUP presence reassure you - still look for ectopic.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Standard ectopic + no IUP = methotrexate option if criteria met. Heterotopic = must use surgery, methotrexate contraindicated due to IUP. Always consider heterotopic in IVF patients.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if β-hCG was positive but nothing was seen on ultrasound?
whatIfScenarios.description: Positive pregnancy test, empty uterus, no adnexal mass - pregnancy of unknown location

chiefComplaint.original: I have really bad stomach pain on the right side
chiefComplaint.modified: Positive pregnancy test, β-hCG 1200, but ultrasound shows empty uterus and no adnexal mass

feedback.correct: PUL with β-hCG below discriminatory zone (~1500-2000): cannot diagnose location yet. Check serial β-hCG in 48 hours. Normal IUP: β-hCG doubles. Ectopic: rises slowly or plateaus. Miscarriage: falls. Keep patient safe, repeat imaging when β-hCG reaches discriminatory zone.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of PUL. What's the follow-up plan?
feedback.incorrect: Pregnancy of Unknown Location: β-hCG positive but too early to see on US (below discriminatory zone ~1500-2000). Cannot distinguish early IUP from ectopic. Serial β-hCG q48h: should rise >53% if viable IUP. Plateau or slow rise suggests ectopic. Strict precautions and follow-up.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Visible ectopic = can diagnose and treat. PUL = too early to localize, need serial β-hCG. Key is ensuring patient returns for follow-up and knows warning signs of rupture.

female-35-heavy.oldcartsNarrative: "My periods have been really heavy for about 6 months. I'm going through a super tampon and pad every hour on my heaviest days. I pass clots the size of golf balls. It lasts 8-9 days now instead of 5. I feel exhausted all the time. I've noticed my belly looks bigger - my pants are tighter. I have to pee more often. The periods are regular, just really heavy. I've never been pregnant. No pain really, just heavy bleeding. I'm starting to miss work because I'm so tired."

historyQuestions.text: How heavy is the bleeding? How many pads/tampons per day?
historyQuestions.answer: On my heaviest days, I'm using a super tampon AND a pad, changing every hour. I've ruined so many clothes and sheets. I use about 30 pads per cycle.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Changing pad/tampon hourly or >80mL per cycle = heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia).

historyQuestions.text: Are you passing blood clots?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, big ones. Like golf balls sometimes. It's frightening.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Clots larger than a quarter indicate heavy bleeding.

historyQuestions.text: How long does your period last?
historyQuestions.answer: 8-9 days now. It used to be 5.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged menses (>7 days) is part of heavy menstrual bleeding definition.

historyQuestions.text: Are your periods regular or irregular?
historyQuestions.answer: Regular - every 28 days like clockwork. Just really heavy now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Regular heavy bleeding = structural cause (fibroids, adenomyosis). Irregular suggests ovulatory dysfunction.

historyQuestions.text: Could you be pregnant? When was your last period?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm not sexually active right now. Last period ended 2 weeks ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must rule out pregnancy even if patient says 'no' - still get test.

historyQuestions.text: Are you experiencing fatigue or shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm exhausted. Can barely make it through the day. I get winded walking up stairs now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Symptoms of anemia - this patient is symptomatic and needs Hgb checked urgently.

historyQuestions.text: Any pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, or constipation?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I'm peeing all the time now. And I feel pressure, like something is there.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bulk symptoms from large fibroids pressing on bladder and rectum.

historyQuestions.text: Any pelvic pain or pain with periods?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really painful, just heavy. Maybe some cramping on heavy days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Adenomyosis usually causes dysmenorrhea along with heavy bleeding.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor indicates anemia from chronic blood loss.

physicalExam.finding: Tired-appearing woman. Pale skin and conjunctivae. No distress.

physicalExam.reasoning: Large fibroid uterus palpable abdominally. Size measured in 'weeks' as if pregnant.

physicalExam.finding: Cervix appears normal. No cervical lesions. Small amount of blood in vaginal vault.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out cervical pathology as source of bleeding.

physicalExam.finding: Uterus enlarged to 18-week size, irregular contour ('lumpy bumpy'), firm, mobile, non-tender. Cervix firm. Adnexae not well palpated due to uterine size.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic fibroid findings: enlarged, irregular, firm uterus.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.reasoning: ALWAYS FIRST in any reproductive-age woman with abnormal bleeding. Pregnancy complications (threatened abortion, ectopic) can present as 'heavy period.' Never skip this.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: Hgb 8.2 g/dL (LOW, normal 12-16), Hct 26%, MCV 72 fL (microcytic), RDW 16.5% (elevated), WBC 6.8, Plt 385,000 (mildly elevated - reactive)
labs.reasoning: Chronic blood loss causes iron deficiency anemia. Microcytosis (low MCV) and high RDW are classic. Thrombocytosis can be reactive to iron deficiency or blood loss.

labs.name: Iron Studies (Ferritin, Serum Iron, TIBC)
labs.result: Ferritin 8 ng/mL (LOW, normal >20), Serum Iron 25 mcg/dL (low), TIBC 450 mcg/dL (high), % saturation 6% (low)
labs.reasoning: Confirms iron deficiency. Low ferritin is most sensitive marker. High TIBC and low saturation are classic pattern. Patient needs iron replacement.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction is common cause of AUB. Hypothyroidism causes heavy bleeding (menorrhagia). Hyperthyroidism causes light/irregular bleeding. Always check TSH.

labs.name: PT/PTT
labs.result: PT 12.5 sec, INR 1.0, PTT 28 sec - normal coagulation
labs.reasoning: Consider coagulopathy workup if: heavy bleeding since menarche, family history of bleeding disorders, other bleeding symptoms (epistaxis, bruising). von Willebrand disease is most common inherited bleeding disorder.

labs.name: von Willebrand panel
labs.result: Consider if heavy bleeding since menarche or bleeding history
labs.reasoning: vWD affects up to 13% of women with heavy menstrual bleeding. Screen with: vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), Factor VIII. Consider for young women with lifelong heavy periods.

imaging.name: Pelvic Ultrasound (transvaginal preferred + transabdominal)
imaging.result: TRANSVAGINAL/TRANSABDOMINAL: Uterus enlarged measuring 14 x 10 x 8 cm. Multiple fibroids identified: Dominant intramural fibroid 7 cm in posterior wall. Submucosal fibroid 3 cm (FIGO Type 1, >50% intracavitary) distorting endometrial cavity. Several smaller intramural fibroids (2-3 cm). Endometrial stripe 6 mm (proliferative phase, appropriate). Ovaries: Right 3.2 x 2.1 cm with normal follicles. Left 2.8 x 2.0 cm, normal. No free fluid.
imaging.reasoning: FIRST-LINE imaging for AUB. Transvaginal provides best resolution of endometrium and submucosal fibroids. Transabdominal needed for large uteri extending above pelvis. Confirms fibroid location, number, and relationship to endometrial cavity.

imaging.name: Saline Infusion Sonohysterography (SIS)
imaging.result: Saline instillation demonstrates 3 cm submucosal fibroid, FIGO Type 1, with >50% protrusion into endometrial cavity. Remaining cavity normal. No polyps.
imaging.reasoning: Better characterizes intracavitary lesions than standard ultrasound. Saline distends cavity allowing visualization of submucosal fibroids and polyps. Helps determine resectability for hysteroscopic surgery.

imaging.name: MRI Pelvis
imaging.result: Consider for complex cases or pre-procedural planning (UAE, myomectomy)
imaging.reasoning: MRI best for: mapping multiple fibroids before myomectomy, distinguishing fibroids from adenomyosis, ruling out leiomyosarcoma (rare). Not first-line but useful for surgical planning.

procedures.name: Endometrial Biopsy (Pipelle)
procedures.result: Proliferative phase endometrium. No hyperplasia, atypia, or malignancy identified.
procedures.reasoning: ACOG recommends EMB for AUB in women: ≥45 years, OR <45 with risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, PCOS, chronic anovulation, unopposed estrogen, tamoxifen, family history). This patient is 35 with regular cycles - EMB optional but reasonable to rule out pathology.

procedures.name: Diagnostic/Operative Hysteroscopy
procedures.result: Consider for direct visualization and potential same-session treatment of submucosal fibroid or polyps
procedures.reasoning: Hysteroscopy allows direct visualization of cavity and can be therapeutic (resect submucosal fibroids, polyps). For Type 0 or 1 submucosal fibroids, hysteroscopic myomectomy is definitive treatment.

treatments.name: Iron supplementation
treatments.result: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO twice daily on empty stomach with vitamin C for absorption. Recheck CBC in 4-6 weeks. Target Hgb >12.
treatments.reasoning: ALL patients with iron deficiency anemia need iron replacement. Oral iron: ferrous sulfate, fumarate, or gluconate. GI upset common - can take with food if needed (reduces absorption). IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if oral intolerant or severe anemia.

treatments.name: Hormonal management options
treatments.result: Options: Combined OCP (reduces bleeding 40-50%), Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena - reduces bleeding 80-90%), Progestin-only pills or depot. Consider if desires contraception or not surgical candidate.
treatments.reasoning: Hormonal options are first-line for many patients. Mirena IUD is highly effective for menorrhagia. OCPs regulate cycles and reduce flow. Depot medroxyprogesterone also reduces bleeding. Choice depends on contraception needs and contraindications.

treatments.name: Tranexamic acid (Lysteda)
treatments.result: Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO TID for first 5 days of period. Reduces bleeding by 30-50%.
treatments.reasoning: Non-hormonal option. Antifibrinolytic reduces menstrual blood loss. Take only during menses. Contraindicated with history of thrombosis. Can use with hormonal methods.

treatments.name: NSAIDs during menses
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600-800 mg TID or Naproxen 500 mg BID during menses. Reduces bleeding 20-40%.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandins involved in menstrual bleeding and cramping. Modest effect on flow. Good for patients with dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia. Start 1-2 days before anticipated menses.

treatments.name: GnRH agonist (Lupron) or antagonist
treatments.result: Consider for preoperative fibroid shrinkage or as temporizing measure
treatments.reasoning: GnRH agonists create hypoestrogenic state, shrink fibroids ~50%. Used preoperatively to reduce surgical blood loss and fibroid size. Limited to 6 months due to bone loss. Add-back therapy recommended.

consults.name: Gynecology referral
consults.result: Refer to gynecology for surgical options discussion and management
consults.reasoning: Symptomatic fibroids causing anemia warrant gynecology referral. Surgical options include: hysteroscopic myomectomy (for submucosal), abdominal/laparoscopic myomectomy, uterine artery embolization, hysterectomy.

consults.name: Interventional Radiology (for UAE)
consults.result: Consider uterine artery embolization as uterine-sparing alternative to surgery
consults.reasoning: UAE is effective for symptomatic fibroids. Preserves uterus but may affect fertility. Contraindicated if pregnancy desired. Discuss as option for patients wanting to avoid surgery.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with GYN referral
disposition.result: Start iron supplementation, trial of hormonal management if desired, GYN referral for surgical options discussion.
disposition.reasoning: AUB and fibroids managed outpatient. Expedited GYN referral for symptomatic fibroids causing anemia. Consider transfusion only for severe symptomatic anemia.

actualDiagnosis.name: Symptomatic Uterine Leiomyomas (Fibroids) with Iron Deficiency Anemia
teachingPoints: AUB-PALM COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy; Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
Submucosal fibroids cause most bleeding (distort cavity); intramural less so; subserosal least
Quantify bleeding: PBAC score, # pads, presence of clots >1 inch, flooding
Treatment options: medical (hormonal, tranexamic acid, NSAIDs) vs procedural (hysteroscopic myomectomy, UAE, hysterectomy)
Iron supplementation for all with iron deficiency
Fibroids shrink after menopause - expectant management reasonable if near menopause

female-35-heavy.teachingPoint: This is a bread-and-butter GYN case. Key learning points: 1) ALWAYS pregnancy test first, 2) Quantify the bleeding, 3) Check hemoglobin - patients adapt to anemia, 4) Ultrasound confirms diagnosis, 5) Treatment depends on fertility desires, symptoms, and fibroid location. This patient has a submucosal fibroid amenable to hysteroscopic resection if she desires fertility. If not, options include hormonal management, UAE, or hysterectomy.
female-35-heavy.clinicalPearl: Know your fibroid terminology for rounds: Submucosal = protrudes into uterine cavity (causes bleeding), Intramural = within myometrium (can cause bulk/bleeding if large), Subserosal = protrudes outside uterus (causes bulk symptoms, less bleeding), Pedunculated = on a stalk (can tort). The FIGO submucosal classification (Type 0, 1, 2) guides hysteroscopic resectability.

mcqs.question: Which discharge is MOST consistent with bacterial vaginosis?

options.text: Thick, white, cottage cheese-like, with itching
Thin, gray-white, fishy odor, minimal itching
options.explanation: Classic BV: thin homogeneous discharge, fishy amine odor (especially with KOH), pH >4.5.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Vaginosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Thin discharge, fishy odor, elevated pH.

allDiagnoses.name: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Has itching - but discharge character more consistent with BV.

allDiagnoses.name: Trichomoniasis

female-22-discharge.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this discharge for about a week. It's grayish-white and has a smell - kind of fishy. More itching than usual, mostly external. No real pain. I'm sexually active with my boyfriend - we've been together 3 months. We use condoms sometimes. My last period was 2 weeks ago. No pain during sex. I've had yeast infections before but this seems different."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the discharge - color, consistency, amount.
historyQuestions.answer: It's grayish-white, kind of thin and watery. More than usual. It stains my underwear.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Thin, homogeneous, gray-white discharge is classic BV.

historyQuestions.text: Any odor?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, kind of fishy. Especially after sex.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC: Fishy amine odor, worse after intercourse (semen is alkaline, releases amines).

historyQuestions.text: Any itching or irritation?
historyQuestions.answer: Some itching, mostly on the outside. Not as bad as when I've had yeast infections.
historyQuestions.reasoning: BV causes minimal itching. Intense itching is more yeast.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your sexual activity.
historyQuestions.answer: I've been with my current boyfriend for 3 months. We have sex a couple times a week. We use condoms maybe half the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inconsistent condom use = STI risk. Must test.

historyQuestions.text: Any symptoms in your partner?
historyQuestions.answer: He hasn't said anything. I don't think so.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Male partners are often asymptomatic, especially with trich.

historyQuestions.text: Any pain with sex?
historyQuestions.answer: No, sex doesn't hurt.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Deep dyspareunia might suggest PID.

historyQuestions.text: Any fever or lower abdominal pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever, no belly pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever and lower abdominal pain would suggest PID.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Afebrile, vital signs normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Afebrile argues against PID.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No lower abdominal tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: No abdominal tenderness - PID less likely.

physicalExam.finding: Normal vulva. No erythema, no lesions, no excoriations. Minimal external irritation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lack of vulvar inflammation argues against yeast.

physicalExam.finding: Thin, homogeneous, grayish-white discharge coating vaginal walls. No clumping. Vaginal walls not erythematous. Cervix appears normal - no discharge from os, no friability. FISHY ODOR noted.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic BV appearance. Cervix looks healthy - argues against cervicitis.

physicalExam.finding: Clue cells present (>20%). No trichomonads seen. Rare WBCs. KOH whiff test positive (fishy odor). pH 5.0 (elevated).
physicalExam.reasoning: Amsel criteria met: (1) thin homogeneous discharge, (2) pH >4.5, (3) positive whiff test, (4) clue cells. 3 of 4 = BV.

physicalExam.finding: No cervical motion tenderness. Uterus normal size, non-tender. Adnexae non-tender, no masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: IMPORTANT: No cervical motion tenderness rules out PID.

labs.name: GC/Chlamydia NAAT
labs.reasoning: Must test - young, sexually active, inconsistent condoms. NAAT is gold standard for GC/CT screening.

labs.name: Trichomonas NAAT
labs.reasoning: NAAT more sensitive than wet mount for trich. Wet mount only 60-70% sensitive; NAAT >95%.

labs.name: HIV screening
labs.reasoning: Should be offered to all sexually active patients. BV increases HIV acquisition risk 60%.

labs.name: Urine pregnancy test
labs.reasoning: Affects treatment choices. BV in pregnancy increases preterm birth risk - always treat.

labs.name: Wet mount microscopy
labs.result: Clue cells >20%, rare WBCs, no trichomonads, no yeast/pseudohyphae
labs.reasoning: Clue cells are vaginal epithelial cells covered with bacteria giving stippled appearance. Pathognomonic for BV.

labs.name: Vaginal pH testing
labs.reasoning: pH >4.5 is one of Amsel criteria. Lactobacilli normally maintain acidic pH; BV shifts to alkaline.

labs.name: KOH whiff test
labs.reasoning: Adding KOH releases volatile amines (putrescine, cadaverine). One of Amsel criteria.

labs.name: RPR/Syphilis screening
labs.reasoning: Consider in patients with STI risk factors. Part of comprehensive STI screening.

labs.name: Hepatitis B surface antigen
labs.reasoning: Consider in patients with multiple partners or inconsistent condom use.

labs.name: HSV testing (if lesions present)
labs.reasoning: Only if genital ulcers present. Don't test asymptomatic patients routinely.

treatments.name: Metronidazole 500mg PO BID x 7 days
treatments.result: First-line treatment initiated. Counseled to avoid alcohol during treatment and for 24h after (disulfiram-like reaction).
treatments.reasoning: First-line for BV. 90-95% cure rate. Must counsel about alcohol interaction - causes severe nausea, vomiting, flushing.

treatments.name: Metronidazole 0.75% gel intravaginally x 5 days
treatments.result: Alternative to oral. Applied at bedtime for 5 nights.
treatments.reasoning: Equivalent efficacy to oral. May be preferred if GI intolerance to oral or patient preference. Less systemic absorption = less alcohol interaction.

treatments.name: Clindamycin 2% cream intravaginally x 7 days
treatments.result: Alternative treatment if metronidazole contraindicated.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative first-line. Note: Can weaken latex condoms for 5 days after use. Not recommended for pregnant women (associated with preterm birth in some studies).

treatments.name: Tinidazole 2g PO x 2 days OR 1g PO x 5 days
treatments.result: Alternative oral treatment.
treatments.reasoning: Nitroimidazole like metronidazole. Similar efficacy. May have better GI tolerance but more expensive.

treatments.name: Secnidazole 2g PO single dose
treatments.result: Single-dose treatment completed in office.
treatments.reasoning: FDA-approved single-dose option. Granules mixed with applesauce, yogurt, or pudding. Good for adherence concerns.

treatments.name: Lactobacillus probiotics (adjunctive)
treatments.result: Discussed as potential adjunct to reduce recurrence.
treatments.reasoning: Evidence is mixed. May help restore normal vaginal flora. Not a substitute for antibiotics but may reduce recurrence.

consults.name: Partner notification counseling
consults.result: Counseled that BV is NOT an STI - partner treatment NOT recommended. However, if STI tests return positive, partner must be treated.
consults.reasoning: BV is NOT an STI - partner treatment doesn't improve outcomes or reduce recurrence. Different from trich where partner Rx is mandatory.

consults.name: OB/GYN referral
consults.result: Not needed for uncomplicated BV. Would refer if recurrent (3+ episodes/year) or treatment failure.
consults.reasoning: Primary care can manage uncomplicated BV. Refer if recurrent (consider suppressive therapy) or diagnostic uncertainty.

disposition.name: Discharge with follow-up instructions
disposition.result: Discharged with metronidazole prescription. Return if symptoms persist after completing treatment. Will call with STI results.
disposition.reasoning: Most BV is treated outpatient. Follow-up only needed if symptoms persist or STI results positive.

disposition.name: STI results follow-up plan
disposition.result: Will receive call in 24-48h with GC/CT results. If positive, will need treatment and partner notification.
disposition.reasoning: Must have system to follow up positive results. GC/CT requires treatment and expedited partner therapy.

disposition.name: Recurrence prevention counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that BV recurs in ~30% within 3 months. Limiting douching, consistent condom use, and limiting new partners may help.
disposition.reasoning: Recurrence is common. Modifiable risks: douching (disrupts flora), multiple partners, inconsistent condoms. Can consider suppressive therapy if recurrent.

disposition.name: Alcohol avoidance counseling
disposition.result: IMPORTANT: No alcohol during metronidazole treatment and for 24 hours after completing course - causes severe reaction.
disposition.reasoning: Metronidazole + alcohol = disulfiram-like reaction (severe nausea, vomiting, flushing, headache, tachycardia). Must emphasize this.

disposition.name: Safe sex counseling
disposition.result: Counseled about consistent condom use to reduce STI risk. BV itself increases susceptibility to STIs including HIV.
disposition.reasoning: BV disrupts vaginal barrier function and increases acquisition of HIV, HSV, HPV, GC, CT. Consistent condoms protective.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return if: fever, lower abdominal pain, symptoms worsen or don't improve after treatment, new symptoms develop.
disposition.reasoning: Fever/abdominal pain could indicate PID (especially if GC/CT positive). Treatment failure may need alternative regimen or re-evaluation.

BV is NOT an STI but is associated with sexual activity
Treatment: Metronidazole 500mg PO BID x 7 days OR metronidazole gel 0.75% intravaginally x 5 days
Partner treatment NOT needed for BV (unlike trich)
BV increases susceptibility to HIV and other STIs

female-22-discharge.teachingPoint: This is classic BV - know your vaginitis differentials for OB/GYN! Quick reference: BV = thin gray, fishy, pH >4.5, clue cells. Yeast = thick white cottage cheese, intense itching, normal pH, pseudohyphae. Trich = yellow-green frothy, strawberry cervix, motile trichomonads, treat partner. Always test for STIs in sexually active patients - don't assume the clinical diagnosis is complete.
female-22-discharge.clinicalPearl: The 'sniff test' or whiff test: Add KOH to the discharge. In BV, this releases volatile amines that smell fishy. Patients often report the odor is worse after sex because semen is alkaline. Also know that BV during pregnancy increases preterm birth risk - always treat in pregnant patients.

historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes my belly gets hard for a few seconds, maybe a few times a day, but it doesn't hurt and it goes away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Painless, irregular contractions = Braxton Hicks (normal). Regular, painful contractions = possible preterm labor.

historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.

labs.result: 124 mg/dL (normal <140 mg/dL). Passed - no gestational diabetes.
labs.reasoning: UNIVERSAL screening for GDM at 24-28 weeks. Give 50g glucose load, check blood sugar at 1 hour. Cutoff varies (130-140 mg/dL). If elevated → 3-hour GTT to confirm.

labs.result: WBC 9.8, Hgb 11.2 g/dL (mild physiologic anemia), Hct 33.8%, MCV 88, Plt 245,000
labs.reasoning: Screen for anemia. Physiologic anemia of pregnancy (hemodilution) is expected, but Hgb <10 warrants iron supplementation. This Hgb 11.2 is acceptable.

labs.name: Urine dipstick for protein
labs.result: Negative for protein, negative for glucose, negative for leukocytes.
labs.reasoning: Screen for proteinuria (preeclampsia) at every prenatal visit. Also screens for glycosuria (GDM) and UTI.

labs.name: Antibody screen (indirect Coombs)
labs.reasoning: Repeat antibody screen at 28 weeks, especially if Rh-negative or history of transfusion. Identifies antibodies that could cause hemolytic disease of newborn.

imaging.name: Anatomy ultrasound review (done at 20 weeks)
imaging.result: Reviewed prior anatomy scan: Normal anatomy, posterior placenta, AFI 14cm (normal), single fetus, no anomalies.
imaging.reasoning: Review anatomy scan results (typically done 18-22 weeks). No repeat imaging needed at 28 weeks if prior scan was normal.

imaging.name: Growth ultrasound (if indicated)
imaging.result: Not indicated - fundal height matches gestational age.
imaging.reasoning: Growth ultrasound indicated if: fundal height discrepancy >3cm, high-risk pregnancy, or prior growth abnormality.

procedures.name: Fundal height measurement
procedures.result: Fundal height = 28 cm, appropriate for 28 weeks.
procedures.reasoning: Fundal height (cm) should equal gestational age (weeks) ±3cm after 20 weeks. Discrepancy warrants ultrasound for dating or growth assessment.

procedures.name: Fetal heart rate by Doppler
procedures.reasoning: Confirm fetal cardiac activity at each visit. Normal FHR: 110-160 bpm.

treatments.name: RhoGAM (if Rh-negative)
treatments.result: Not indicated - patient is Rh positive. If Rh-negative, would give RhoGAM 300 mcg IM at 28 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: RhoGAM at 28 weeks prevents Rh sensitization. Give to ALL Rh-negative mothers. Repeat after delivery if baby is Rh-positive.

treatments.name: Tdap vaccine
treatments.result: Tdap vaccine administered today (28 weeks is within optimal 27-36 week window).
treatments.reasoning: Tdap recommended between 27-36 weeks (optimally 27-32 weeks) each pregnancy. Protects newborn from pertussis through maternal antibody transfer.

treatments.name: Influenza vaccine (if in season)
treatments.result: Flu vaccine offered. Patient agreed to receive.
treatments.reasoning: Flu vaccine recommended for all pregnant women during flu season. Safe in any trimester.

treatments.name: Continue prenatal vitamins
treatments.result: Continue prenatal vitamins with folic acid and DHA daily.
treatments.reasoning: Prenatal vitamins throughout pregnancy. Folic acid for neural tube defect prevention, iron for anemia prevention.

treatments.name: Iron supplementation (if anemic)
treatments.result: Not currently indicated. Hgb 11.2 is acceptable. Would supplement if Hgb <10.
treatments.reasoning: Iron supplementation for Hgb <10-11. Ferrous sulfate 325mg daily. Take with vitamin C for absorption.

disposition.name: Routine prenatal follow-up
disposition.result: Return in 2 weeks (at 30 weeks). Then every 2 weeks until 36 weeks, then weekly until delivery.
disposition.reasoning: Standard prenatal visit schedule: every 4 weeks until 28 weeks, every 2 weeks until 36 weeks, then weekly.

disposition.name: Teach fetal kick counts
disposition.result: Instructed on kick counts: Starting at 28 weeks, count fetal movements daily. Goal: 10 movements in 2 hours. Call if decreased movement.
disposition.reasoning: Kick counts are simple, free, and help detect fetal distress. Decreased movement warrants evaluation (NST, BPP).

disposition.name: Review warning signs
disposition.result: Reviewed danger signs requiring immediate call/visit: vaginal bleeding, fluid leakage, decreased fetal movement, severe headache, visual changes, severe abdominal pain, regular contractions before 37 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Every visit: reinforce warning signs. Patients must know when to call or come in urgently.

disposition.name: Discuss upcoming GBS culture
disposition.result: Discussed: GBS vaginal/rectal swab will be done at 36 weeks to determine need for intrapartum antibiotics.
disposition.reasoning: Group B Strep culture at 36-37 weeks. If positive, IV penicillin in labor to prevent neonatal GBS disease.

disposition.name: Begin birth plan discussion
disposition.result: Discussed patient's preferences for labor and delivery. Will continue discussion at future visits.
disposition.reasoning: Start discussing birth preferences: pain management (epidural vs unmedicated), support persons, cord clamping, skin-to-skin, breastfeeding.

disposition.name: Encourage hospital tour/registration
disposition.result: Encouraged to register at hospital and take tour if available.
disposition.reasoning: Practical preparation for delivery.

disposition.name: Discuss infant feeding plans
disposition.result: Discussed breastfeeding benefits and offered lactation resources.
disposition.reasoning: Early discussion of feeding plans allows education and preparation.

actualDiagnosis.name: Normal Intrauterine Pregnancy at 28 Weeks Gestation
teachingPoints: 28-WEEK VISIT CHECKLIST: Glucose screen (GCT), CBC, Rh status check, RhoGAM if needed, urine protein, Tdap discussion
GLUCOSE SCREENING: 1-hour GCT is universal at 24-28 weeks. Cutoff varies (130-140 mg/dL). If elevated, 3-hour GTT.
RhoGAM TIMING: Give at 28 weeks to Rh-negative mothers AND within 72 hours of delivery if baby is Rh-positive
FUNDAL HEIGHT: Should equal gestational age ±3cm. Discrepancy suggests dating error, growth restriction, or macrosomia
FETAL MOVEMENT: Teach kick counts in third trimester. 10 movements in 2 hours is reassuring.
WARNING SIGNS TO REPORT: Bleeding, fluid leakage, decreased fetal movement, severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, severe edema
TDAP VACCINE: Recommended 27-36 weeks (ideally 27-32 weeks for optimal antibody transfer)
VISIT FREQUENCY: Every 4 weeks until 28 weeks, every 2 weeks until 36 weeks, then weekly

primi-28wks.teachingPoint: This is a routine 28-week prenatal visit - one of the most important visits because of the screenings performed. The 28-week checklist: (1) Glucose challenge test for GDM - this patient passed with 124 mg/dL, (2) CBC to check for anemia - her Hgb 11.2 is acceptable physiologic anemia, (3) Rh status confirmation and RhoGAM if Rh-negative - she's Rh+ so not needed, (4) Urine dipstick for protein - negative rules out preeclampsia, (5) Discuss Tdap vaccine - should be given between 27-36 weeks. Key counseling: teach kick counts, review warning signs, discuss birth plan, address questions. Schedule GBS culture at 36 weeks.
primi-28wks.clinicalPearl: Prenatal care follows a predictable pattern that medical students should know: First visit (8-10 weeks) - dating, baseline labs, counseling. 11-14 weeks - first trimester screening option. 15-20 weeks - quad screen if not NIPT. 18-22 weeks - anatomy ultrasound. 24-28 weeks - glucose screen, RhoGAM, Tdap. 36 weeks - GBS culture. Remember the RED FLAGS patients must report immediately: vaginal bleeding, fluid leakage, decreased fetal movement, severe headache, visual changes, severe abdominal pain, signs of labor before 37 weeks.

threatened-abortion.name: Amanda L.

missedConsequences.Ectopic Pregnancy: Ruptured ectopic can cause fatal hemorrhage. Must rule out with ultrasound in any pregnant patient with bleeding.
missedConsequences.Missed Abortion: Retained products of conception can lead to infection (septic abortion), hemorrhage.
missedConsequences.Molar Pregnancy: Can progress to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia if not properly managed.

commonPitfalls: Assuming bleeding always means miscarriage - up to 50% of threatened abortions result in viable pregnancy
Not ruling out ectopic in every case of first trimester bleeding
Relying on a single β-hCG level - trend is more important than absolute value
Forgetting Rh status and RhoGAM in all bleeding patients
Not providing emotional support - this is a frightening experience for patients

allDiagnoses.name: Threatened Abortion
allDiagnoses.rationale: First trimester bleeding with closed cervix and documented fetal cardiac activity. Most common diagnosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Subchorionic Hemorrhage
allDiagnoses.rationale: Blood collection between chorion and uterine wall - common cause of first trimester bleeding with good prognosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: MUST rule out with ultrasound in every case of first trimester bleeding. Life-threatening if ruptures.

allDiagnoses.name: Molar Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would see 'snowstorm' appearance on US, very high β-hCG. Can become malignant.

allDiagnoses.name: Inevitable/Incomplete Abortion
allDiagnoses.rationale: If cervix is open, miscarriage is inevitable. Incomplete requires management.

allDiagnoses.name: Cervical Cause (polyp, ectropion)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Post-coital bleeding from cervical pathology. Bleeding not from uterus.

threatened-abortion.oldcartsNarrative: "I noticed some spotting 2 days ago when I wiped - it was brownish. Yesterday it got a little heavier and turned more red. I've been using a pad and it's not soaking through, maybe just spotting the pad. I had some mild cramping yesterday but it's better today. This is my first pregnancy - I'm about 8 weeks based on my last period. I found out I was pregnant 3 weeks ago. I'm so worried - does this mean I'm losing the baby?"

historyQuestions.text: How much bleeding have you had? How many pads?
historyQuestions.answer: Just spotting really. I've been using pads but they're barely getting stained. Maybe one pad a day with some spots on it. It's not like a heavy period.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Quantifying blood loss is essential. Spotting is different from soaking pads. Heavy bleeding with clots suggests more significant pathology.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about any cramping or pain.
historyQuestions.answer: I had some cramping yesterday, kind of like mild period cramps. It's actually better today. No sharp or severe pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mild cramping with spotting can be normal. Severe or unilateral pain raises concern for ectopic.

historyQuestions.text: Have you passed any tissue or clots?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. Just blood.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Passage of tissue suggests abortion in progress. May need to examine any passed tissue.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period? How sure are you of the dates?
historyQuestions.answer: My last period started November 1st. I'm pretty sure because we were trying to get pregnant so I was tracking everything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Accurate dating is crucial. LMP of Nov 1 makes her about 8 weeks - should see fetal heartbeat by now on ultrasound.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any ultrasounds so far this pregnancy?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my first appointment was supposed to be next week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior ultrasound means ectopic hasn't been ruled out yet. Must confirm intrauterine pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had pregnancy symptoms - nausea, breast tenderness?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been really nauseous, especially in the mornings. My breasts are sore. I've been exhausted.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ongoing pregnancy symptoms are reassuring. Sudden loss of symptoms can indicate failed pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been pregnant before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, this is my first pregnancy. We tried for over a year.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior ectopic or miscarriage increases risk. First pregnancy - no prior obstetric history.

historyQuestions.text: Do you know your blood type?
historyQuestions.answer: I think I'm O negative? My mom told me I'm the universal donor.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rh-negative patients need RhoGAM with any pregnancy bleeding to prevent isoimmunization.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 116/74, HR 78, RR 14, Temp 98.4°F. Hemodynamically stable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Stable vital signs are reassuring. Tachycardia or hypotension would indicate significant blood loss.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No guarding or rebound. No palpable masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Peritoneal signs would suggest ruptured ectopic - surgical emergency.

physicalExam.finding: Small amount of dark blood in vaginal vault. Cervix appears closed. No active bleeding from cervical os. Cervix without lesions.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLOSED CERVIX is key finding - allows diagnosis of threatened abortion. Open cervix = inevitable abortion.

physicalExam.finding: Uterus slightly enlarged, approximately 8-week size, anteverted. Mild tenderness to palpation. No adnexal masses or tenderness. No cervical motion tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Uterus appropriate for dates. No adnexal mass or CMT makes ectopic less likely (but doesn't rule it out).

labs.name: Quantitative β-hCG
labs.reasoning: Confirms pregnancy, correlates with ultrasound findings. At β-hCG >1500-2000 (discriminatory zone), TVUS should show gestational sac if IUP. This level is well above threshold.

labs.name: Blood type and Rh
labs.reasoning: Confirms Rh negative status - MUST give RhoGAM to prevent isoimmunization. This is critical and often forgotten.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline hemoglobin important if bleeding worsens. No anemia currently.

labs.name: Repeat β-hCG in 48 hours (if diagnosis uncertain)
labs.result: Not needed - US confirmed viable IUP. Would check if ectopic or nonviable pregnancy suspected.
labs.reasoning: In viable IUP, β-hCG should roughly double every 48h. If diagnosis clear on US, repeat not needed.

imaging.name: Transvaginal Ultrasound (DIAGNOSTIC)
imaging.result: Intrauterine gestational sac with yolk sac and fetal pole. Crown-rump length 14mm consistent with 7w6d. Fetal cardiac activity present at 156 bpm (normal). Small subchorionic hemorrhage measuring 1.5 x 0.8 cm. Cervix closed, length normal. No adnexal masses. No free fluid.
imaging.reasoning: CRITICAL test. Confirms: (1) Intrauterine pregnancy (rules out ectopic), (2) Fetal viability (cardiac activity), (3) Explains bleeding (subchorionic hemorrhage). This is reassuring.

procedures.name: RhoGAM 300 mcg IM
procedures.result: Given in deltoid. Prevents Rh isoimmunization.
procedures.reasoning: ALL Rh-negative patients with pregnancy bleeding MUST receive RhoGAM within 72 hours to prevent sensitization. Do not forget this.

procedures.name: Pelvic examination
procedures.result: Speculum: Cervix closed, small amount old blood in vault. Bimanual: Uterus 8-week size, no adnexal masses.
procedures.reasoning: CERVICAL STATUS IS KEY: Closed = threatened abortion. Open = inevitable abortion. Must document.

treatments.name: Reassurance (primary treatment)
treatments.result: Extensively counseled: Fetal heartbeat present is excellent sign. >95% of threatened abortions with visible FCA result in viable pregnancy. Subchorionic hemorrhage usually resolves.
treatments.reasoning: With FCA present, prognosis is excellent. Reassurance is the main 'treatment' - nothing medical can prevent miscarriage if it's going to happen.

treatments.name: Pelvic rest
treatments.result: Advised to avoid intercourse and strenuous activity until bleeding stops and follow-up confirms ongoing viability.
treatments.reasoning: Standard recommendation for threatened abortion. No evidence it prevents miscarriage, but reasonable precaution.

treatments.name: RhoGAM administration
treatments.result: RhoGAM 300 mcg IM given (patient is Rh negative).
treatments.reasoning: Prevents Rh sensitization. Must give to ALL Rh-negative patients with bleeding regardless of pregnancy outcome.

treatments.name: Progesterone supplementation (controversial)
treatments.result: Not routinely indicated. May be considered if history of recurrent pregnancy loss or documented low progesterone.
treatments.reasoning: Progesterone supplementation is controversial in threatened abortion. PRISM trial showed modest benefit in women with prior miscarriage. Not standard for first threatened abortion.

treatments.name: No strict bed rest required
treatments.result: Bed rest NOT required. Can continue normal activities. Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous exercise.
treatments.reasoning: Strict bed rest does NOT prevent miscarriage and is not recommended. Normal activity is fine.

consults.name: OB/GYN (for follow-up)
consults.result: Follow-up ultrasound in 1-2 weeks to confirm ongoing viability and resolution of subchorionic hemorrhage.
consults.reasoning: Follow-up imaging ensures continued fetal viability. Most subchorionic hemorrhages resolve.

disposition.name: Discharge home with follow-up
disposition.result: Discharged home. Viable IUP confirmed. RhoGAM given. Follow-up in 1-2 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Hemodynamically stable with confirmed viable IUP - outpatient management appropriate.

disposition.name: Follow-up ultrasound in 1-2 weeks
disposition.result: Repeat ultrasound scheduled in 10-14 days to confirm ongoing viability, appropriate growth, and resolution of subchorionic hemorrhage.
disposition.reasoning: Repeat imaging confirms fetal viability and tracks subchorionic hemorrhage. Usually resolves by end of first trimester.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return immediately for: heavy bleeding (soaking pad in <1 hour), severe abdominal pain, passing tissue or clots, fever, dizziness/fainting.
disposition.reasoning: Clear return precautions are essential. Heavy bleeding or tissue passage indicates miscarriage in progress.

disposition.name: Emotional support and counseling
disposition.result: Acknowledged patient's fear and anxiety. Reassured that fetal heartbeat is excellent prognostic sign. Offered resources for support.
disposition.reasoning: This is a frightening experience. Acknowledge emotions, provide appropriate reassurance, offer support resources.

disposition.name: Set expectations
disposition.result: Counseled: Spotting may continue for days to weeks. Subchorionic hemorrhages typically resolve. Nothing we do can prevent miscarriage if it's going to happen - but the odds are in her favor.
disposition.reasoning: Set realistic expectations. Spotting may persist. Cannot guarantee outcome, but prognosis is good.

disposition.name: Activity instructions
disposition.result: Pelvic rest (no intercourse) until follow-up. Normal activities otherwise. No strenuous exercise. Can work.
disposition.reasoning: Practical guidance on activity restrictions.

disposition.name: Continue prenatal vitamins
disposition.result: Continue prenatal vitamins with folic acid.
disposition.reasoning: Routine prenatal care should continue.

actualDiagnosis.name: Threatened Abortion with Subchorionic Hemorrhage - Viable Pregnancy
keyFindings: Viable intrauterine pregnancy confirmed on ultrasound
teachingPoints: THREATENED ABORTION DEFINITION: First trimester bleeding with CLOSED cervix and viable pregnancy
KEY DIAGNOSTIC STEPS: (1) Confirm IUP on ultrasound, (2) Check fetal cardiac activity, (3) Check Rh status
SUBCHORIONIC HEMORRHAGE: Common cause of first trimester bleeding. Prognosis generally good if FCA present.
β-hCG AND ULTRASOUND: At β-hCG >1500-2000, should see gestational sac on TVUS. If not, suspect ectopic.
RhoGAM: Give to ALL Rh-negative patients with pregnancy bleeding, regardless of outcome
PROGNOSIS: With visible fetal cardiac activity, ~95% will continue to viable pregnancy
PATIENT COUNSELING: Pelvic rest (no intercourse), return for heavy bleeding/severe pain/fever, nothing prevents miscarriage if it's going to happen
EMOTIONAL SUPPORT: This is frightening - acknowledge feelings, provide reassurance when appropriate

threatened-abortion.teachingPoint: This case illustrates the approach to first trimester bleeding: (1) Assess hemodynamic stability, (2) Speculum exam to assess cervical os - OPEN vs CLOSED determines diagnosis, (3) Ultrasound to confirm intrauterine pregnancy and fetal viability, (4) Check Rh status. This patient has all the good prognostic signs: closed cervix, visible fetal cardiac activity, and only a small subchorionic hemorrhage. With FCA present, >95% of threatened abortions will result in viable pregnancy. Key management: RhoGAM (she's Rh-negative), pelvic rest, and reassurance. There's nothing we can do to prevent miscarriage if it's destined to happen, but most of these do well.
threatened-abortion.clinicalPearl: The terminology of early pregnancy loss can be confusing. Here's the breakdown: THREATENED = bleeding but closed os, viable fetus (this case). INEVITABLE = open os, pregnancy will be lost. INCOMPLETE = some tissue passed, some retained. COMPLETE = all tissue passed. MISSED = fetal demise but no bleeding yet (will see on US). SEPTIC = any of above plus infection. The os tells you everything - if it's open, miscarriage is inevitable regardless of what the ultrasound shows.
