young-woman-options.name: Taylor R.
young-woman-options.chiefComplaint: I want to discuss birth control options
young-woman-options.variantDescription: Nulliparous woman seeking long-term reliable contraception
commonPitfalls: Not asking about contraindications before recommending hormonal methods
Forgetting to ask about plans for future pregnancy (timing matters for method choice)
Not discussing LARC methods (IUDs, implant) as first-line options - they're most effective
Assuming nulliparous women can't use IUDs - this is outdated thinking
Not screening for STI risk - contraception doesn't equal STI protection
Imposing personal values on contraceptive choices

options.note: Surgical, considered permanent, regret is common in young women
Outpatient procedure, most effective permanent method

allDiagnoses.name: Contraception Counseling - LARC Candidate
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young woman seeking reliable contraception without immediate pregnancy plans - excellent LARC candidate.

allDiagnoses.name: Undiagnosed Contraindication to Estrogen
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must screen for migraine with aura, VTE history, smoking over 35 before recommending combined hormonal contraception.

allDiagnoses.name: STI Risk
allDiagnoses.rationale: Contraception counseling is opportunity to discuss STI prevention. Most contraceptives don't protect against STIs.

young-woman-options.oldcartsNarrative: "I'm here to talk about birth control. I've been using condoms with my boyfriend but I'm worried about getting pregnant. We've been together for 2 years and I'm not planning to have kids for at least 5-6 years - I want to finish grad school and get established in my career first. I tried the pill in college but I was terrible at remembering to take it every day. I've heard about IUDs but I'm a little scared. My periods are regular, maybe a little heavy. No major health problems."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your plans for having children.
historyQuestions.answer: Definitely want kids eventually, but not for at least 5-6 years. I'm starting grad school next fall and I want to be established in my career first.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps guide method choice. Long timeline → LARC is ideal. Short timeline → avoid Depo (delayed fertility return).

historyQuestions.text: What methods have you used before? How did they work for you?
historyQuestions.answer: I tried the pill for about a year in college but I kept missing doses and had a pregnancy scare. Since then just condoms, which makes me nervous.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poor compliance with daily pill suggests methods not requiring daily action (LARC, patch, ring, Depo) would be better.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your periods - are they regular? Heavy? Painful?
historyQuestions.answer: Pretty regular, every 28-30 days. They're kind of heavy - I go through a lot of tampons. Some cramping but Advil helps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy periods → hormonal IUD would help (lighter periods). Copper IUD would likely worsen heavy periods.

historyQuestions.text: Do you get headaches or migraines? Specifically, migraines with visual changes or aura?
historyQuestions.answer: I get occasional headaches but nothing major. No migraines or visual stuff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL screening question. Migraine WITH AURA is absolute contraindication to estrogen-containing contraception (stroke risk).

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke cigarettes?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never have.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking over age 35 is contraindication to estrogen. Not relevant here but must always ask.

historyQuestions.text: Have you or anyone in your family had blood clots?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that in my family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Personal or strong family history of VTE is contraindication to estrogen-containing methods.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm pretty healthy. Just seasonal allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screen for conditions affecting method choice: breast cancer, liver disease, etc.

historyQuestions.text: Are you and your partner in a mutually monogamous relationship? Any concerns about STIs?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, we've been exclusive for 2 years. We both got tested when we started dating.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low STI risk - but still important to discuss that most contraceptives don't protect against STIs.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned being scared of IUDs - what concerns do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: I've heard they hurt to put in, and what if it moves or something? Also I thought you couldn't get them if you haven't had kids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Address misconceptions. IUDs are safe and recommended for nulliparous women. Insertion is brief discomfort.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/72, Weight 145 lbs, BMI 24.2
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal BP allows combined hormonal options. Normal BMI - no weight-based contraindications.

physicalExam.finding: No masses, no nipple discharge, no skin changes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Breast exam not required before contraception initiation but may be done at preventive visit.

physicalExam.finding: Normal external genitalia. Vagina without lesions. Cervix appears normal. Uterus anteverted, normal size, non-tender. No adnexal masses or tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pelvic exam is NOT required to start most contraception but IS needed before IUD insertion.

physicalExam.finding: Cervix appears normal, os visible. No cervical motion tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: If planning IUD, assess cervix. CMT would need STI testing before insertion.

labs.name: Urine pregnancy test
labs.reasoning: Must rule out pregnancy before starting any contraceptive method. This is the ONLY required test for most methods.

labs.name: Chlamydia/Gonorrhea testing (if indicated)
labs.result: Offered. Patient declines given monogamous relationship. Can be done at IUD insertion if desired.
labs.reasoning: STI testing recommended but doesn't need to delay IUD insertion in low-risk asymptomatic patients. Test and treat same day is acceptable.

labs.name: Blood pressure
labs.reasoning: Required before estrogen-containing contraception (pill, patch, ring). Normal BP allows these options.

labs.name: Pap smear (if due)
labs.result: Not due - age <21 or done within past 3 years.
labs.reasoning: Pap smear is NOT required before contraception. Follow age-appropriate cervical cancer screening guidelines (start at 21).

procedures.name: IUD insertion (if chosen)
procedures.result: Patient elected Mirena IUD. Insertion scheduled for today. Will perform pelvic exam, sound uterus, insert device.
procedures.reasoning: IUD insertion is office procedure. Takes 5-10 minutes. Brief cramping during insertion. Can be done same day as decision.

procedures.name: Implant insertion (alternative)
procedures.result: Would insert in non-dominant arm subdermally. Takes 5 minutes. Local anesthesia used.
procedures.reasoning: Implant (Nexplanon) is inserted subdermally in upper arm. Quick office procedure. Can be palpated to confirm placement.

treatments.name: Hormonal IUD (Mirena/Liletta) - RECOMMENDED
treatments.result: Mirena IUD selected and inserted. Levonorgestrel-releasing IUD, effective 7 years. Will help with heavy periods.
treatments.reasoning: Excellent choice for this patient: long-acting (5-7 years), highly effective (99.8%), will reduce her heavy periods by up to 90%, reversible immediately upon removal.

treatments.name: Smaller hormonal IUD alternative (Kyleena/Skyla)
treatments.result: Could use Kyleena (lower hormone, 5 years) or Skyla (3 years) if concerned about side effects, but less impact on heavy periods.
treatments.reasoning: Smaller IUDs may have easier insertion in nulliparous women. Less menstrual suppression than Mirena.

treatments.name: Copper IUD (Paragard) - alternative
treatments.result: Discussed but not ideal for this patient - would likely worsen her already heavy periods.
treatments.reasoning: Copper IUD is hormone-free, lasts 10 years, but INCREASES menstrual bleeding. Not ideal for patients with heavy periods.

treatments.name: Implant (Nexplanon) - alternative
treatments.result: Discussed as alternative. Most effective method available. Main concern is unpredictable bleeding patterns.
treatments.reasoning: Implant is most effective contraceptive (99.95%). Lasts 3 years. Main side effect is irregular bleeding which some find bothersome.

treatments.name: NSAIDs for insertion discomfort
treatments.result: Recommended ibuprofen 600-800mg 30-60 minutes before IUD insertion.
treatments.reasoning: Pre-medication with NSAIDs may reduce insertion discomfort. Evidence is mixed but commonly recommended.

treatments.name: Backup contraception instructions
treatments.result: Hormonal IUD is effective immediately if inserted within 7 days of period start. Otherwise backup for 7 days.
treatments.reasoning: Timing matters: If inserted during menses or within 7 days of period start, effective immediately. Otherwise, backup method for 7 days.

disposition.name: Same-day IUD insertion
disposition.result: IUD inserted today. Procedure well-tolerated with mild cramping. Strings visualized and trimmed.
disposition.reasoning: Same-day insertion is recommended when possible - improves uptake. Don't make patients come back for insertion.

disposition.name: Post-insertion instructions
disposition.result: Expect cramping for 24-48 hours. Take ibuprofen as needed. Spotting may occur for first few months. Can use tampons.
disposition.reasoning: Set expectations: cramping is normal, irregular bleeding/spotting common for 3-6 months, then typically much lighter or absent periods.

disposition.name: String check instructions
disposition.result: Instructed on monthly string check - feel for strings at cervix. Can return if unable to feel strings.
disposition.reasoning: Patient should check strings monthly. Unable to feel strings may indicate expulsion (rare) and warrants evaluation.

disposition.name: Warning signs to return for
disposition.result: Return for: severe pelvic pain, fever, abnormal discharge (possible PID), unable to feel strings, missing period with positive pregnancy test.
disposition.reasoning: Warning signs: signs of infection (fever, discharge, severe pain) or possible pregnancy (very rare). Expulsion most likely in first few months.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: String check visit in 4-6 weeks optional. Return PRN for concerns. No routine visits needed otherwise.
disposition.reasoning: String check visit is optional but reassuring. No required follow-up if doing well.

disposition.name: Effectiveness and duration counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: Mirena >99% effective, lasts up to 7 years, immediately reversible. Fertility returns immediately upon removal.
disposition.reasoning: Important counseling: highly effective, long-lasting but reversible, fertility returns immediately when removed.

disposition.name: STI prevention reminder
disposition.result: Reminded that IUD does NOT prevent STIs. Use condoms if at risk for STI exposure.
disposition.reasoning: Critical point: IUD prevents pregnancy but NOT STIs. Dual method (IUD + condoms) if STI risk.

actualDiagnosis.name: Contraception Counseling - LARC Recommended
keyFindings: Heavy periods (favors hormonal IUD over copper)
teachingPoints: LARC METHODS ARE FIRST-LINE per ACOG: Most effective, highest continuation rates, cost-effective
NULLIPARITY IS NOT A CONTRAINDICATION TO IUDs: This is outdated guidance. IUDs are safe and recommended for all ages including adolescents
US MEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria): Category 1 = no restriction, Category 2 = benefits > risks, Category 3 = risks usually > benefits, Category 4 = unacceptable risk
ESTROGEN CONTRAINDICATIONS (Category 4): Migraine with aura, smoking if ≥35 years, VTE history, breast cancer, certain heart conditions
HEAVY PERIODS: Hormonal IUD (Mirena) reduces menstrual bleeding by 90% - therapeutic benefit. Copper IUD may worsen bleeding.
IMPLANT: Most effective contraceptive available (99.95%). Main issue is irregular bleeding.
SHARED DECISION MAKING: Present all options, elicit patient preferences, respect autonomy
STI PROTECTION: Only condoms (male and female) provide STI protection. Consider dual method if at risk.

young-woman-options.teachingPoint: This patient is an ideal candidate for LARC (Long-Acting Reversible Contraception). She wants reliable contraception for 5+ years, had compliance issues with the pill, and has no contraindications. Best options: (1) Hormonal IUD (Mirena, Liletta) - would also help her heavy periods, lasts 5-7 years, (2) Implant (Nexplanon) - most effective method available, lasts 3 years, (3) Copper IUD - if she wanted hormone-free, but might worsen her heavy periods. Key counseling points: Address her misconceptions about IUDs in nulliparous women (they're safe and recommended), explain that insertion involves brief cramping but is quick, and discuss that IUDs don't prevent STIs. The only exam needed before initiating most contraception is a pregnancy test - pelvic exam is needed before IUD but can be done at insertion visit.
young-woman-options.clinicalPearl: Remember the US MEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use) categories: Category 1 = Use freely, Category 2 = Generally use (benefits > risks), Category 3 = Usually don't use (risks usually > benefits), Category 4 = Don't use (unacceptable risk). The most common Category 4 contraindication is MIGRAINE WITH AURA for estrogen-containing methods. Always ask specifically about aura (visual changes, sensory symptoms, speech difficulty before headache) - regular migraines without aura are Category 2 only. Also remember: you do NOT need a pelvic exam to prescribe pills, patch, ring, or Depo - pregnancy test is sufficient.

uncomplicated-pregnancy.name: Maya R.
uncomplicated-pregnancy.variantDescription: First pregnancy with comprehensive prenatal care through uncomplicated vaginal delivery

longitudinalDays.title: Week 8: First Prenatal Visit
longitudinalDays.presentation: 28-year-old G1P0 woman presents for first prenatal visit. LMP was 6 weeks ago. Home pregnancy test positive 2 weeks ago.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 8 weeks ago. My periods are usually pretty regular, every 28-30 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: LMP establishes gestational age.

historyQuestions.text: Any symptoms so far?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been really tired. Some breast tenderness. A little queasy in the mornings.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fatigue, breast tenderness, and mild nausea are expected.

historyQuestions.text: Any vaginal bleeding or spotting?
historyQuestions.answer: No bleeding at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of bleeding is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been pregnant before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, this is my first pregnancy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: G1P0. First pregnancy - nulliparous.

historyQuestions.text: Any medications, supplements, or drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin. I stopped drinking alcohol and coffee when I found out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Appropriate behavior modification.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/72, HR 82. BMI 22.9 (normal weight).
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal BMI. Target weight gain 25-35 lbs.

physicalExam.finding: Cervix closed, bluish discoloration (Chadwick sign). Uterus slightly enlarged.
physicalExam.reasoning: Chadwick sign = early pregnancy sign.

labs.name: Prenatal Panel
labs.result: Blood type A+. Antibody screen negative. Hgb 13.2. Rubella immune. RPR NR. HBsAg neg. HIV neg.
labs.reasoning: Standard first prenatal labs.

labs.name: Urinalysis and culture
labs.reasoning: Screen for asymptomatic bacteriuria.

imaging.name: Dating ultrasound
imaging.result: Single IUP. CRL consistent with 8w2d. FHR 165 bpm.

treatments.name: Prenatal vitamins with folic acid
treatments.result: Rx: Prenatal vitamin with 800mcg folic acid daily.
treatments.reasoning: Folic acid prevents neural tube defects.

disposition.name: Schedule follow-up visits
disposition.result: Next visit at 12 weeks. Visits q4 weeks until 28 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Standard prenatal visit schedule.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: First prenatal visit: Dating US, prenatal labs, genetic screening discussion, folic acid, patient education.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 20: Anatomy Scan
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya returns at 20 weeks for anatomy scan. Feeling well. Nausea resolved. Feeling quickening.
intervalHistory: NIPT at 10 weeks: Low risk for trisomies. Fetal sex: Female
Feeling fetal movement for past week

historyQuestions.text: When did you first feel the baby move?
historyQuestions.answer: About a week ago. Little flutters, like butterflies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Quickening at 18-20 weeks for primigravida.

physicalExam.finding: Fundal height 20 cm (at umbilicus).
physicalExam.reasoning: Fundal height in cm should equal gestational age in weeks.

imaging.name: Anatomy ultrasound
imaging.result: Single female fetus. All anatomy normal. Placenta anterior, not previa. AFI normal.

disposition.name: Schedule GTT at 24-28 weeks
disposition.result: 1-hour GTT scheduled for 26 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Universal GDM screening.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Anatomy scan at 18-22 weeks evaluates all fetal structures, placenta, and amniotic fluid.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 28: Start of Third Trimester
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya at 28 weeks. Glucose test results available. Baby moving frequently.

historyQuestions.text: How is the baby moving?
historyQuestions.answer: Very active! Especially after I eat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Active fetal movement is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Any swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: My ankles swell a bit at the end of the day, but it goes away overnight.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dependent edema that resolves overnight is normal.

labs.name: 1-hour GTT result
labs.reasoning: Passed GDM screen.

treatments.name: Tdap vaccine
treatments.result: Tdap administered.
treatments.reasoning: Tdap between 27-36 weeks transfers pertussis antibodies to baby.

disposition.name: Teach fetal kick counts
disposition.result: Count 10 movements in 2 hours after meals.
disposition.reasoning: Kick counts starting at 28 weeks.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: 28 weeks: GDM screen, Tdap, RhoGAM if Rh negative, start kick counts, q2 week visits.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 36: Approaching Term
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya at 36 weeks. Pelvic pressure increasing. Discussing birth plan.

physicalExam.finding: Vaginal-rectal swab collected for GBS.
physicalExam.reasoning: GBS culture at 36-37 weeks.

physicalExam.finding: Cervix 1 cm dilated, 50% effaced, -2 station, vertex.
physicalExam.reasoning: Early cervical change is normal.

labs.name: GBS culture
labs.result: Results pending. POSITIVE result received 48 hours later.
labs.reasoning: If positive, IV penicillin in labor.

treatments.name: Review labor signs
treatments.result: Come for: regular contractions q5 min x1 hour, rupture of membranes, decreased movement, bleeding.
treatments.reasoning: Clear instructions for when to come to hospital.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: GBS screening at 36-37 weeks. If positive, IV penicillin during labor prevents neonatal GBS disease.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 39: Labor and Delivery
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya at 39+2 weeks with regular contractions for 4 hours. SROM at home - clear fluid.

historyQuestions.text: Describe your contractions.
historyQuestions.answer: Every 4 minutes, lasting about a minute. Getting stronger.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Regular, intensifying contractions = true labor.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your water breaking.
historyQuestions.answer: Gush of clear fluid about an hour ago. Keeps leaking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Clear fluid = normal. Green/brown = meconium.

physicalExam.finding: 5 cm dilated, 100% effaced, 0 station, vertex.
physicalExam.reasoning: 5 cm = active labor.

physicalExam.finding: FHR baseline 140, moderate variability, accelerations present. Category I.
physicalExam.reasoning: Category I = normal, reassuring.

treatments.name: GBS prophylaxis
treatments.result: Penicillin G 5 million units IV, then 2.5 million q4h.
treatments.reasoning: GBS positive - IV penicillin until delivery.

disposition.name: Admit to L&D
disposition.result: Active labor. GBS prophylaxis started.
disposition.reasoning: Active labor with SROM - admission.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Labor: Primigravida dilates ~1 cm/hour in active labor. GBS prophylaxis given at least 4 hours before delivery.

longitudinalDays.title: Delivery
longitudinalDays.presentation: After 1 hour 47 minutes of pushing, Maya delivers a healthy baby girl via SVD.
longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: Third stage: Deliver placenta within 30 minutes. Active management with pitocin prevents PPH.

longitudinalDays.title: Postpartum Day 1
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya postpartum day 1. Baby latching well. Some perineal discomfort.

historyQuestions.text: How is your bleeding?
historyQuestions.answer: Like a heavy period.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Moderate lochia rubra is normal.

historyQuestions.text: How are you feeling emotionally?
historyQuestions.answer: Happy but tired. A little weepy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baby blues common days 3-5. Watch for PPD.

physicalExam.finding: Fundus firm, at umbilicus.
physicalExam.reasoning: Fundus descends ~1 cm/day.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: PPD1 checklist: Fundus firm? Lochia moderate? Voiding? Pain controlled? Breastfeeding? Mood?

longitudinalDays.title: 6-Week Postpartum Visit
longitudinalDays.presentation: Maya at 6-week postpartum. Baby doing well. Breastfeeding exclusively.

historyQuestions.text: How are you feeling emotionally?
historyQuestions.answer: Much better. First 2 weeks were hard but now I feel like myself.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screen all postpartum patients for depression.

historyQuestions.text: Are you interested in contraception?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I want to wait a couple years before another baby.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Contraception counseling is essential.

labs.name: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale
labs.reasoning: Screen all postpartum patients.

treatments.name: Contraception initiation
treatments.result: Mirena IUD inserted today.
treatments.reasoning: IUD safe with breastfeeding and highly effective.

longitudinalDays.teachingPoint: 6-week visit: Complete exam, PPD screening, contraception counseling, cleared for exercise and intercourse.

guidelineReferences.name: CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021 - PID
guidelineReferences.citation: Workowski KA, et al. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2021;70(4):1-187
keyPoints: Clinical diagnosis - low threshold for treatment
Outpatient: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BID x 14 days + Metronidazole

mild-moderate-pid.name: Jasmine W.
mild-moderate-pid.chiefComplaint: Lower belly pain and discharge for 5 days

diagnosticCriteria.minimum: Pelvic or lower abdominal pain + cervical motion OR uterine OR adnexal tenderness

allDiagnoses.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young, sexually active, lower abdominal pain, discharge, CMT, adnexal tenderness - classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: MUST get pregnancy test. Ruptured ectopic is life-threatening.

allDiagnoses.name: Tubo-Ovarian Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Complication of PID. Would have mass on exam/imaging, higher fever, more ill-appearing.

allDiagnoses.name: Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: RLQ pain in women - always on differential. Different character of pain, no discharge.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden onset, severe, colicky. Would have adnexal mass.

allDiagnoses.name: Uncomplicated Cervicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have discharge but not abdominal pain, tenderness, or systemic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Urinary Tract Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have dysuria, frequency, urgency. Different exam findings.

mild-moderate-pid.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had lower belly pain for about 5 days. It's getting worse and it hurts on both sides now. I have this gross discharge - yellow-green color, kind of smells. Sex has been painful lately. I've had some bleeding between periods. I feel a little feverish. I'm sexually active with a new partner - we've been together about 2 months. We usually use condoms but not every time."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the pain - where, how bad, what makes it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: It's in my lower belly, on both sides. Aching and crampy. Maybe 6 out of 10. Moving around and sex make it worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilateral lower abdominal pain worsened by movement suggests peritoneal irritation.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the discharge.
historyQuestions.answer: Yellow-greenish, kind of thick. It smells bad - not normal for me. Started about the same time as the pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Purulent vaginal discharge suggests cervicitis/PID.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 weeks ago. I've had some spotting between periods though, which is unusual.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Intermenstrual bleeding can occur with PID (endometritis). Must rule out pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your sexual history and partners.
historyQuestions.answer: I have a new boyfriend - we've been together 2 months. He's my only current partner. We use condoms but not every time. I've had maybe 5 partners total.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New partner, inconsistent condom use = STI risk. Multiple lifetime partners increases risk.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been tested or treated for STIs?
historyQuestions.answer: I had chlamydia once in college and took antibiotics for it. Nothing since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior chlamydia = risk factor for PID and for having chlamydia again.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had pain with intercourse?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, for the last week or so. Deep pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Deep dyspareunia suggests upper tract involvement.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have an IUD or use any contraception?
historyQuestions.answer: No IUD. Just condoms when we use them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IUD placement within 3 weeks increases PID risk. Not a factor here.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever supports infectious/inflammatory process.

physicalExam.finding: Lower abdominal tenderness bilaterally, worse on the right. No rebound but mild guarding. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bilateral tenderness typical for PID. Right-sided predominance doesn't exclude PID but consider appendicitis.

physicalExam.finding: Mucopurulent discharge from cervical os. Cervix friable (bleeds easily when touched). Vaginal discharge yellow-green.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mucopurulent cervical discharge is one of the supportive criteria for PID diagnosis.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE cervical motion tenderness. Bilateral adnexal tenderness, right greater than left. Uterine tenderness present. No adnexal masses palpable.
physicalExam.reasoning: CMT + adnexal tenderness + uterine tenderness = meets minimum criteria for PID diagnosis.

physicalExam.finding: >10 WBC per high-power field on vaginal fluid wet mount.
physicalExam.reasoning: Increased WBCs on wet mount support PID diagnosis and is one of the additional criteria.

labs.name: Urine pregnancy test
labs.reasoning: MUST rule out pregnancy before treating PID. Ectopic pregnancy can have similar presentation - negative test rules it out as explanation for symptoms.

labs.name: GC/CT NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test)
labs.result: Pending (results in 1-3 days, will be positive for chlamydia)
labs.reasoning: Test for causative organisms. Don't wait for results to treat - PID is a clinical diagnosis. NAAT can be from endocervical swab or urine.

labs.name: HIV test
labs.reasoning: STI workup should include HIV testing per CDC. Offer with any STI diagnosis.

labs.name: RPR for syphilis
labs.reasoning: Full STI screening with any STI diagnosis. Syphilis rates are rising.

labs.name: Hepatitis B surface antigen, Hepatitis C antibody
labs.reasoning: Consider hepatitis screening in high-risk individuals.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis supports inflammatory process. Marked elevation would suggest more severe infection.

labs.name: ESR and CRP
labs.reasoning: Elevated inflammatory markers are supportive criteria for PID diagnosis. Not required for diagnosis.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Trace WBC, no bacteria, no blood
labs.reasoning: Helps rule out UTI as alternative diagnosis. Mild pyuria can be present in PID from urethral inflammation.

imaging.name: Pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal preferred)
imaging.result: Not required for mild-moderate PID with typical presentation.
imaging.reasoning: US not required to diagnose uncomplicated PID. INDICATIONS: severe illness, adnexal mass on exam, not responding to treatment (to evaluate for TOA), uncertain diagnosis. Findings in PID: thickened/fluid-filled tubes, free pelvic fluid, TOA if present.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Not indicated for typical PID presentation
imaging.reasoning: Consider if surgical emergency (appendicitis) cannot be excluded or if TOA suspected. Otherwise, clinical diagnosis is sufficient.

procedures.name: Culdocentesis
procedures.reasoning: Historical procedure - rarely used now. Was used to detect purulent fluid or blood in cul-de-sac.

procedures.name: Diagnostic laparoscopy
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard for diagnosis but invasive. Reserved for: uncertain diagnosis, no response to treatment, to exclude surgical pathology.

treatments.name: Outpatient treatment (CDC recommended)
treatments.result: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 dose NOW + Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x14 days + Metronidazole 500mg PO BID x14 days
treatments.reasoning: CDC 2021 outpatient regimen covers N. gonorrhoeae (ceftriaxone), C. trachomatis (doxycycline), and anaerobes/BV organisms (metronidazole). Alternative: Cefoxitin 2g IM + Probenecid 1g PO + Doxycycline.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg TID with food for pain and inflammation
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs help with pain and inflammation. Avoid if pregnant (but we ruled out pregnancy).

treatments.name: Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)
treatments.result: Partner treatment discussed - prescription provided for partner to pick up or patient to deliver. Azithromycin 1g + Ceftriaxone 500mg IM.
treatments.reasoning: CDC recommends treating ALL partners in past 60 days. EPT (providing prescription/medication for partners without exam) is legal in most states and improves treatment rates.

treatments.name: Sexual abstinence counseling
treatments.result: Counseled: No intercourse until both patient AND partner complete treatment AND symptoms resolved
treatments.reasoning: Prevents reinfection and transmission. Both partners must complete treatment.

treatments.name: Condom counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on consistent condom use to prevent future STIs
treatments.reasoning: Prevention of future infections. Barrier methods reduce STI transmission.

consults.name: OB/GYN consultation
consults.result: Not required for uncomplicated outpatient PID
consults.reasoning: Consult if: uncertain diagnosis, failed outpatient therapy, TOA present, surgical emergency suspected, pregnant.

disposition.name: Discharge with outpatient treatment
disposition.result: Discharged with prescriptions, follow-up in 48-72 hours, return precautions given.
disposition.reasoning: Mild-moderate PID without inpatient criteria can be managed outpatient. CRITICAL: Must have re-evaluation at 48-72 hours to ensure response to treatment.

disposition.name: Inpatient admission (if indicated)
disposition.result: NOT required for this patient
disposition.reasoning: INPATIENT CRITERIA: Pregnancy, surgical emergency not excluded, failure of oral treatment, severe illness (high fever, nausea/vomiting, peritonitis), TOA present, unable to tolerate PO, immunocompromised, adolescent with compliance concerns.

actualDiagnosis.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (Mild-Moderate) - Outpatient Management
teachingPoints: CDC MINIMUM CRITERIA FOR PID: Pelvic/lower abdominal pain + ONE of: CMT, uterine tenderness, adnexal tenderness
TREAT EMPIRICALLY: Don't wait for GC/CT results. Treatment covers GC, CT, and anaerobes.
OUTPATIENT TREATMENT: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 + Doxycycline 100mg BID x14 days +/- Metronidazole 500mg BID x14 days
INPATIENT CRITERIA: Pregnancy, surgical emergency not excluded, no response to oral treatment, severe illness, TOA, unable to take PO
PARTNER TREATMENT: All partners in past 60 days need treatment, even if asymptomatic
SEQUELAE OF PID: Infertility (tubal scarring), ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain
FOLLOW-UP: Re-evaluate in 48-72 hours if not improving

mild-moderate-pid.teachingPoint: This patient meets criteria for PID: lower abdominal pain + CMT + adnexal tenderness + supportive findings (discharge, fever, elevated WBCs on wet mount). She's well enough for outpatient treatment: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM once + Doxycycline 100mg BID for 14 days + Metronidazole 500mg BID for 14 days. Key counseling: (1) Complete all antibiotics, (2) No sex until treatment complete AND partner treated, (3) Return if worsening, (4) Partner MUST be treated. Long-term consequences of PID include infertility and ectopic pregnancy - this emphasizes importance of treatment and prevention.
mild-moderate-pid.clinicalPearl: You don't need positive GC/CT tests to diagnose and treat PID - many cases are caused by other organisms (anaerobes, G. vaginalis, H. influenzae). The threshold for diagnosing PID is intentionally low because the consequences of untreated PID (infertility, ectopic pregnancy) are severe. If a sexually active young woman has lower abdominal pain and you can elicit pelvic tenderness, treat empirically for PID.
